text.tspan: The US dollar's 10% decline in 2025, its steepest drop
since 1973, reshaped who bid at auction and where,
drawing foreign capital into New York while cooling
American appetite for overseas sales.
Currency moves of 10% to 15% can swing the effective
cost of a major artwork by hundreds of thousands of
dollars, making exchange rates as important as
condition reports or provenance in cross-border deals.
Global art sales reached $59.6 billion in 2025, but that
dollar-denominated figure overstates growth in
local-currency terms for many markets outside the US.
Auction houses price works in multiple currencies, but
catalogue estimates are set weeks before sale day,
creating gaps that informed buyers exploit.
Art investors should treat currency as a real risk factor:
most return figures are reported in US dollars and
embed exchange-rate effects without disclosing them.

svg.text: Does a weak dollar make art cheaper for foreign buyers?

text.tspan: Yes. A 10% drop in the dollar means a European collector
effectively gets a 10% discount on a New York purchase,
before fees.

svg.text: Can currency movements affect art investment returns?

text.tspan: Absolutely. A US investor who buys in London and sells in
New York carries implicit GBP/USD exposure. If the dollar
strengthens, the return gets a lift beyond any change in the
work's sterling price.

svg.text: Should art investors hedge currency risk?

text.tspan: Traditional hedging tools are difficult to apply to art due to
uncertain holding periods and illiquidity. Most investors
manage currency risk through awareness and timing.
